scenario: You raised BTN, BB calls. Flop A‚ô¶7‚ô£2‚ô• (dry, disconnected). You have Q‚ô†J‚ô† (nothing).
question: Should you c-bet this dry ace-high board even with nothing?
options: Yes ‚Äî dry ace-high board favors your raising range; BB's capped range whiffs, high fold equity
explanation: C-bet. The A-7-2 rainbow board hits YOUR range (you have the aces, BB would 3-bet AA/AK) and misses the BB's capped calling range. High fold equity + range advantage = textbook c-bet bluff, even holding QJ. Board texture + range advantage drive this, not your cards.

scenario: You raised, and got THREE callers. Flop 9‚ô•8‚ô•7‚ô£ (very wet, multiway). You have A‚ô†A‚ô¶ (overpair).
question: How does multiway + wet board change your overpair c-bet?
options: Bet for value AND protection ‚Äî but be ready to fold to heavy action; multiway wet boards crush overpairs often
explanation: Number of opponents + vulnerability are key factors. AA is strong, but 9-8-7 two-tone with THREE opponents is dangerous ‚Äî straights, two pair, sets, flush draws all live in those ranges. Hardin: bet for value+protection to deny equity, but your overpair's relative strength is much lower multiway. Don't stack off light. This is exactly the 'overvaluing one pair' trap on a wet multiway board.

scenario: You raised, one caller. Flop K‚ô†K‚ô¶4‚ô£ (paired, dry). You have A‚ô•Q‚ô• (ace-high).
question: Paired dry board, you have ace-high. C-bet or check?
options: C-bet ‚Äî paired dry boards favor the aggressor's range and are great bluff spots; caller rarely has a king, folds most of their range
explanation: C-bet bluff. Paired dry boards (K-K-4) are premium c-bet bluffing spots: the caller rarely holds the trips (few Kx in a flat-call range), your range credibly contains kings and overpairs, and most of their range (small pairs, broadway misses) folds. Board texture + range advantage = bet, despite ace-high.

scenario: You raised BTN, the BB (a calling station, fold-to-cbet 25%) calls. Flop 6‚ô†5‚ô†4‚ô¶ (wet, low). You have A‚ô£K‚ô¶ (two overcards, no pair, no draw).
question: Against a station on a wet low board with AK-high, c-bet?
options: No ‚Äî wet board smashes a station's wide calling range, and they don't fold (25% fold-to-cbet); no fold equity, no made hand. Check/give up
explanation: When NOT to c-bet: low fold equity + board favors their range + you have no made hand/draw. A 6-5-4 board hits a station's wide range hard (any two pair, straight, draw), and a 25% fold-to-cbet means they won't fold. Firing AK-high here has no value (worse doesn't call) and no fold equity (they don't fold). Check. Opponent tendency + board texture override 'AK is pretty.'

scenario: YOUR HAND. You 3-bet B‚ô•B (wait: QJs) and reach a flop A-J-7 with middle pair (the J). You CHECK the flop as the aggressor (pot control). Turn is a blank.
question: You checked flop with middle pair on an ace-high board. Was that check correct, and what now?
options: Correct pot-control check (ace hits caller's range, your J is medium-strength); now you can call one bet or take a delayed thin-value/showdown line
explanation: Based on your real QJs hand. Checking middle pair OOP on an ace-high board is a CORRECT pot-control play ‚Äî the ace smashes the caller's range and your jack is only medium strength. This is a legit delayed-c-bet / showdown-value line, NOT a missed c-bet. The leak in the real hand came later (calling big bets with one pair), not from this check. Checking was right.
